items.html: Identify features of positive mental health and well-being
Identify intersections between Sickle Cell Disease (SCD) between and challenges to mental health and well-being across the lifespan
Distinguish positive and negative coping factors known to help to improve mental health and well-being for individuals with SCD
Identify opportunities for healthcare providers to support positive mental health and reduce stress for patients with SCD

slides.title: What Does Positive Mental Health Look Like?

data.html: Positive mental health or emotional functioning is an essential contributor to the health and well-being of individuals with SCD. With the benefit of research and observations from clinical settings, we understand better than ever the close relationship between mind, mood, cognitive functioning and physical well-being/pain experience.

data.heading: Good mental health and well-being individuals with SCD may be associated with:

items.html: Happiness or life satisfaction levels
Spirituality
Positive outlook
Hope
Thinking and problem-solving
Coping
Adjustment
Degree of social support
Sense of agency or control over life circumstances
Freedom from feelings of stigma regarding their health status

data.heading: Patients may experience or report the above as:

items.html: Having a clear mind and the ability to process information and set goals
Ability to pursue meaningful work and to function on the job
Having language to communicate wants and needs with ease
Experiencing restorative sleep
Access to timely and effective pain relief and preventative care
Having regular contact with a supportive friend community
Having access to reliable resources to comfortably meet financial commitments, secure housing, access to appropriate accommodations in the workplace, and food security

data.html: These are factors to consider when supporting good mental health and well-being for people with SCD.

slides.title: Mental Health: Risks and Resiliencies in Children and Adolescents with SCD

data.html:
There is a growing body of research focusing on mental health in individuals with SCD across the lifespan. So let's look at some of the general trends by age group, starting with Children and Adolescents.

Increased risk of mood, anxiety, attention, and behaviour regulation challenges compared to peers without SCD.

Stress and depression may be worsened by pain severity and frequency of pain episodes.

High risk of overt and 'silent' stroke in the early childhood period may have linkages to behavioural, learning and social emotional sequelae for many youth.

Isolation and disruption in peer relationships is a contributor to mood, anxiety, and social skill difficulties.

Development of independent and self-help skills or 'developmental tasks of adolescence' may be slowed or delayed by interruption of home, school, family, and social routines due to a challenging illness course, including the unpredictable nature of pain episodes.

Body image disturbance and self-esteem challenges may be exacerbated by differences in physical maturation, physical appearance, endurance and stamina levels, and attainment of related milestones compared to peers without SCD.

slides.title: Mental Health Risks for Adults with SCD
Protective Factors for Positive Mental Health in Individuals with SCD

data.html: Individuals living with SCD posses a wealth of resources and resiliencies that can support good mental health and well-being despite risks and challenges faced. Healthcare providers can be intentional about centring and highlighting these assets and protective factors during the care encounter.

data.heading: Providing access to effective, culturally-appropriate psychotherapeutic interventions that teach positive coping skills, including:

items.html: use of proactive pain coping strategies
use of mindfulness and cognitive strategies (realistic thinking; thought restructuring)
employing emotion regulation strategies
support for adaptive activity and physical functioning
engagement in relaxation practice
connection to social support
problem-solving support

data.html: What is the approximate prevalence rate, in percent, for depression and anxiety for adults with SCD?

data.heading: Resources
data.body:
The RBCD Hub's article, Sickle Cell Disease and Mental Health, provides an overview of the links between physical and mental health. https://www.rbcd.ca/blog/sickle-cell-disease-and-mental-health

Another great resource: How can sickle cell disease affect mental health? https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/mental-health-and-sickle-cell-disease

items.html: Mental health is something that everyone possesses
As individuals living with chronic illness, youth and adults with sickle cell disease are at increased risk for mental health concerns, especially mood and anxiety challenges

Living with complications of sickle cell disease can negatively affect mental health and wellbeing, as can encounters with healthcare practitioners and systems that are not well-informed about this condition, the need for timely treatment, and maintains barriers to support and needed resources

There are a number of indicators of positive mental health and well-being that individuals with SCD and their caregivers may identify, including hope, connection to spirituality and social support and positive coping

Conducting timely mental health screening and providing appropriate mental and physical treatment interventions can significantly improve mental health and well-being outcomes for individuals with SCD

Al-Marzouki, A.F., Alrefaie, N.L., Aljohani, N.A., Alandanusi, R.A., et al. (2021).The Prevalence of Depression and Anxiety Among Sickle Cell Disease Patients in King Abdulaziz University Hospital. Cureus, 13,

Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (accessed January 5, 2024). Mental illness and addiction: facts and statistics. https://www.camh.ca/en/driving-change/the-crisis-is-real/mental-health-statistics
Conference Board of Canada, The. Nurturing Minds for Secure Futures: Timely Access to Mental Healthcare for Children and Youth in Canada. Ottawa: The Conference Board of Canada, 2023
Ekinci, Celik, Unal, & Ozer. (2012). Psychiatric problems in children and adolescents with sickle cell disease, based on parent and teacher reports. Turkish Journal of Hematology, 29, 259-264.
https://www.nytimes.com/2023/04/21/well/mind/negative-emotions-mental-health.html
Obsborne, H. (June 30, 2022), How can sickle cell disease affect mental health? Medical News Today, https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/mental-health-and-sickle-cell-disease
Pearson, Janz & Ali (2013). Health at a glance: Mental and substance use disorders in Canada. Statistics Canada Catalogue no. 82-624-X.

Smetanin, P., Stiff, D., Briante, C., Adair, C.E., Ahmad,. S. & Khan, M. (2011). The life and economic impact of major mental illnesses in Canada: 2011-2041. Prepared for the Mental Health Commission of Canada. Toronto: RiskAnalytica.
Wilson, S.R. (2023). Mental Health Disorders Are Prevalent and Influence Outcomes in Patients With Sickle Cell Disease. The Hematologist, 20 (6).
Wilson Schaeffer, JJ, Gil, KM, Burhcinal, M, et al. Depression, disease severity, and sickle cell disease. Journal of Behavioral Medicine, 22, 115-126
